1.pericope_label: Faith or Works of the Law?
1.synthesis: Paul addresses the Galatians as 'foolish' (anoētoi)—before whose eyes Jesus Christ was publicly portrayed as crucified. Did they receive the Spirit by works of the law or by hearing with faith? Having begun by the Spirit, are they now being perfected by the flesh? (Galatians 3:1–3). He who supplies the Spirit and works miracles among them—does he do so by works of the law, or by hearing with faith? (Galatians 3:5). Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness (Galatians 3:6; citing Genesis 15:6). Know then that it is those of faith who are the sons of Abraham (Galatians 3:7). Scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the Gentiles by faith, preached the gospel beforehand to Abraham: 'In you shall all the nations be blessed' (Galatians 3:8; citing Genesis 12:3). Those of faith are blessed along with Abraham; those who rely on works of the law are under a curse (Galatians 3:9–10; citing Deuteronomy 27:26). 'The righteous shall live by faith' (Galatians 3:11; citing Habakkuk 2:4). Christ redeemed them from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for them—'Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree'—so that in Christ Jesus the blessing of Abraham might come to the Gentiles, and that they might receive the promised Spirit through faith (Galatians 3:13–14; citing Deuteronomy 21:23).
1.historical_context: The 'publicly portrayed as crucified' (v.1) refers to Paul's initial gospel proclamation at Galatia: his preaching was a vivid verbal depiction of the crucifixion, not a philosophical argument. The Spirit-experience of the Galatian believers (vv.2–5) is Paul's first argument: they received the Spirit through the hearing of faith, not through circumcision or Torah-observance. The Abraham argument (vv.6–14) is the letter's scriptural foundation: Genesis 15:6 (Abraham believed and it was credited to him as righteousness) predates the Torah by 430 years (v.17) and predates circumcision by at least a chapter (Genesis 17)—demonstrating that the covenant relationship between God and Abraham was established through faith, not law-observance. The Genesis 12:3 citation ('in you shall all the nations be blessed') is read as the proleptic preaching of the gospel to Abraham: the 'nations' (ethnē = Gentiles) were included in the Abrahamic blessing from the beginning. The curse-redemption passage (vv.13–14) applies Deuteronomy 21:23 to the crucifixion: Jesus, hung on a tree, absorbed the curse pronounced on covenant-breakers, so that the blessing promised to Abraham might flow to the Gentiles through him.
1.literary_structure: The argument of vv.1–14 moves through three stages: the experiential argument (Spirit received by faith, not law, vv.1–5), the scriptural argument from Abraham (faith credited as righteousness before circumcision or law, vv.6–9), and the curse-redemption argument (Christ absorbed the law's curse to release the Abrahamic blessing to Gentiles, vv.10–14). The three scriptural citations (Genesis 15:6, Deuteronomy 27:26, Habakkuk 2:4) each make the same point from a different angle: righteousness is by faith (Genesis), law-observance without perfect compliance brings curse (Deuteronomy), the righteous live by faith not law (Habakkuk). The section closes with the promised Spirit as the seal of the Abrahamic blessing received through faith—connecting the Spirit-experience of vv.1–5 to the Abrahamic promise of vv.6–9 through the curse-redemption of vv.10–14.
1.theological_application: 'Having begun by the Spirit, are you now being perfected by the flesh?' (v.3) is the question every generation's church must face whenever it supplements the initial grace of the gospel with religious performance. The Galatian logic—the Spirit initiates, Torah-observance perfects—is the template for every completion-theology: the gospel establishes the relationship, religious duty maintains and advances it. Paul's response is that this logic misunderstands both the beginning and the end: the Spirit is not merely the initiating power but the sustaining and completing power; faith is not merely the entry-point but the continuing mode of the entire Christian life. The Abraham example demonstrates that faith was never a merely preliminary stage: Abraham received the covenant through faith before circumcision and died in faith—the 'perfection' of the covenant life is more faith, not the supplementation of faith by works.
1.christology: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (v.13) is one of Paul's sharpest atonement statements: the curse pronounced by the Torah on covenant-breakers (Deuteronomy 27:26) falls on Jesus when he is 'hanged on a tree' (Deuteronomy 21:23). Christ does not merely bear the consequences of sin in general but specifically absorbs the Torah's own condemnation—the very legal system that the agitators want the Galatians to submit to has pronounced its curse on its practitioners, and Christ has exhausted that curse on their behalf. The result: 'the blessing of Abraham might come to the Gentiles, and that we might receive the promised Spirit through faith' (v.14)—the double purpose of the cross is the release of the Abrahamic blessing and the gift of the Spirit, both mediated through the crucified and risen Christ.

15.synthesis: Paul gives a human example: a covenant ratified by a person cannot be annulled or added to (Galatians 3:15). The promises were made 'to Abraham and to his offspring'—not 'offsprings' (plural) but 'offspring' (singular), which is Christ (Galatians 3:16; citing Genesis 13:15). The law, coming 430 years later, does not annul a covenant previously ratified by God, so as to make the promise void. For if the inheritance comes by the law, it no longer comes by promise; but God gave it to Abraham by a promise (Galatians 3:17–18).
15.historical_context: The 430-year figure (v.17) is from Exodus 12:40 (LXX), the duration of Israel's sojourn in Egypt—placing the Sinai law well after the Abrahamic covenant. The argument that a ratified covenant cannot be subsequently annulled draws on Roman and Greek legal practice regarding the irrevocability of wills and covenants—an argument Paul's audience would recognize from everyday commercial life. The 'offspring' (sperma) singular reading is a midrashic exegesis: the Hebrew and Greek words for 'seed/offspring' are collective singular nouns (like 'crowd' in English), but Paul reads the singular as pointing to the one in whom all the promised blessing is concentrated—Christ. This is typological reading: the Abrahamic promise always had Christ as its ultimate referent, even when it applied collectively to Israel.
15.literary_structure: The legal analogy (vv.15–18) provides the structural argument: promise → law does not cancel promise, because in legal logic a prior agreement cannot be overridden by a later one without the original parties' consent. The christo-typological reading of 'offspring' (v.16) is not Paul's main argument but a supporting move: if the promise was always christologically concentrated, then the law (which came 430 years later) was never meant to redefine who the promise-heirs are. The conclusion (v.18) is the either-or: inheritance is either by promise or by law—not both, because if law-keeping is the mechanism, the promise is void; if the promise is the mechanism, law-keeping is not the condition.
15.theological_application: The priority of promise over law (promise came first; law cannot annul promise) establishes a crucial hermeneutical principle for reading the OT: the covenant with Abraham is the interpretive frame within which the Sinai law must be understood, not vice versa. The law cannot redefine the terms of the Abrahamic covenant because it arrived later and was given for a different purpose (as Paul will explain in vv.19–22). The church that reads the OT with the law as the primary frame and the promise as secondary has inverted the order: the promise to Abraham that 'in your offspring all the nations shall be blessed' (Genesis 22:18) is the gospel's Old Testament form, and everything else—including the law—must be read in light of it.
15.christology: 'The promises were made to Abraham and to his offspring... who is Christ' (v.16) is Paul's claim that the Abrahamic covenant has always been christologically shaped: the one in whom all nations are blessed, the ultimate heir of the promise, is Christ himself. This is not an innovation but a reading-back: the letter to the Hebrews makes the same claim (Abraham 'was looking forward to the city that has foundations,' Hebrews 11:10). Those who are 'in Christ' share the inheritance promised to Christ-the-offspring—they are co-heirs with the one who is the ultimate heir. The inheritance is grace-given (through promise), not earned (through law): Christ received it as the Father's gift, and all who belong to Christ share the gift with him.

19.synthesis: Why then the law? It was added because of transgressions, until the offspring should come to whom the promise had been made—it was put in place through angels by an intermediary. Now an intermediary implies more than one, but God is one (Galatians 3:19–20). Is the law then contrary to the promises of God? Certainly not! For if a law had been given that could give life, then righteousness would indeed be by the law. But the Scripture imprisoned everything under sin, so that the promise by faith in Jesus Christ might be given to those who believe (Galatians 3:21–22). Before faith came, the law was held captive under the law, imprisoned until the coming faith would be revealed. The law was a guardian (paidagōgos) until Christ came, in order that they might be justified by faith. But now that faith has come, they are no longer under a guardian (Galatians 3:23–25).
19.historical_context: The angels-through-intermediary argument (vv.19–20) is drawing on Second Temple Jewish tradition that the Torah was given through angelic mediation (cf. Acts 7:53; Hebrews 2:2; Josephus, Antiquities 15.136)—suggesting the law's authority is derivative and mediated, while the promise to Abraham came directly from God ('God is one,' v.20). The paidagōgos (guardian, v.24) is not a teacher in the modern sense but the slave who accompanied a wealthy family's child to school and back, ensuring his safety and behavior—a position of authority but also of custodial limitation, appropriate only for minors. The period 'until Christ came' is the temporal scope of the paidagōgos's service: the law served a custodial function during the period of Israel's 'minority' that is now ended by the coming of the heir. The 'imprisonment under sin' (v.22) is not the law's failure but its diagnostic function: the law reveals the universal condition of sin so that the promise can be received as what it is—unconditional grace for the hopeless.
19.literary_structure: The law's purpose section (vv.19–25) answers the obvious objection: if the promise antedates and cannot be canceled by the law, why give the law at all? Paul's answer has two parts: the law was added 'because of transgressions' (to expose and multiply the consciousness of sin) and as a 'guardian until Christ' (a temporary custodial function). The negative purpose (exposing sin, imprisoning under condemnation) serves the positive end: so that 'the promise by faith in Jesus Christ might be given to those who believe' (v.22). The coming of faith (Christ's faithfulness in his death and resurrection, and/or the believers' responsive faith) ends the paidagōgos's service: the heir has come of age, the guardian is dismissed.
19.theological_application: The law's temporary, custodial purpose is one of Paul's most significant hermeneutical contributions: the Torah was not the final form of God's purpose but a transitional arrangement preparing for the fulfillment in Christ. The paidagōgos metaphor prevents two errors: undervaluing the law (it is not useless—it served a real function in Israel's formation) and overvaluing it (it is not the ultimate goal—it was always pointing beyond itself to the promised offspring). The church that lives 'under the guardian' after the guardian's service has ended has misread both the law and Christ: the law itself pointed to Christ and was fulfilled in him; to return to law-keeping as the mode of covenant standing is to go back under a custody that has been ended by the coming of the heir. The law served its purpose; the promise is now fully available in Christ.
19.christology: 'The Scripture imprisoned everything under sin, so that the promise by faith in Jesus Christ might be given to those who believe' (v.22)—the law's diagnostic work (exposing universal sinfulness) serves the christological end: the promise is given precisely to those who have no self-generated claim on it, who have been 'imprisoned under sin' and have no escape through law-performance. Christ is the fulfillment of the promise, the end of the custody period, the liberating heir whose coming dissolves the guardian's authority. 'Now that faith has come, we are no longer under a guardian' (v.25)—'faith came' is Paul's way of describing the historical event of Christ's faithfulness in death and resurrection, which inaugurated the era of promise-fulfillment. The era of law-custody is over; the era of the Spirit and faith has arrived.

26.synthesis: For in Christ Jesus they are all sons of God, through faith. As many as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ (Galatians 3:26–27). There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is no male and female, for they are all one in Christ Jesus. And if they are Christ's, then they are Abraham's offspring, heirs according to promise (Galatians 3:28–29).
26.historical_context: The triple pair (Jew/Greek, slave/free, male/female) in v.28 corresponds to three fundamental social hierarchies of the ancient world: ethnic identity, social status, and gender. The formula may have been a baptismal proclamation used in the early church (the same triad appears in different forms in 1 Corinthians 12:13 and Colossians 3:11). The 'put on Christ' (v.27) is a clothing metaphor: at baptism, the believer 'wears' Christ as identity-garment, which means that in the community's social life, Christ is the primary identity-marker, displacing the three hierarchical pairs. The conclusion (v.29) is the letter's legal argument in miniature: if they are Christ's and Christ is the Abrahamic offspring, then they are the Abrahamic offspring and heirs of the promise—regardless of circumcision status, ethnic identity, or social position.
26.literary_structure: Verses 26–29 are the climax of the chapter's argument: sons of God through faith (v.26) → baptismal incorporation into Christ (v.27) → dissolution of hierarchical distinctions in Christ (v.28) → inheritance through promise (v.29). The argument circles back to the Abrahamic promise of v.8 ('in you shall all the nations be blessed'): the 'all nations' of the promise find their concrete realization in the community that transcends ethnic, social, and gender hierarchies through incorporation into the one who is the Abrahamic offspring. The 'sons of God' designation applies to all believers, including Gentiles—the son-of-God language used of Israel is now applied to the multi-ethnic community of faith.
26.theological_application: 'There is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (v.28) is perhaps Paul's most radical social statement—and it is not a program for social reform but a description of what has happened in Christ. The social distinctions have not been abolished externally (Jews are still Jews; slaves are still slaves; men and women remain distinct) but they have lost their function as markers of differential access to God and differential standing in the community. In Christ, all are sons, all are heirs, all are equally Abraham's offspring—the criteria of standing before God are faith and incorporation into Christ, not ethnicity, status, or gender. This is simultaneously the most inclusive and the most demanding statement in the NT: every human distinction that privileges some and disadvantages others in the community's common life must be evaluated in light of the 'all one in Christ Jesus' reality.
26.christology: 'You are all one in Christ Jesus' (v.28) is the social form of the christological reality: the 'one Christ' is the one in whom all the distinctions that structure human social life have been relativized by incorporation into his body. 'Putting on Christ' at baptism means that Christ is now the believer's primary social identity—more determinative than ethnicity, legal status, or gender. The 'Abraham's offspring' conclusion (v.29) ties everything together: Christ is the offspring (v.16), believers are in Christ (v.27), therefore believers are Abraham's offspring—not by ethnic descent or proselyte conversion (circumcision) but by faith-incorporation into the one who is the offspring. The entire argument about justification, law, and promise resolves into this christological identity: to be in Christ is to be everything the promise promised.
